name: Peña Trevinca Loop from Laguna de los Peces
name_original: Ascensión al Peña Trevinca desde la Laguna de los Peces – circular por el parque natural Lago de Sanabria y sierras Segundera y de Porto
description: Hike the difficult Peña Trevinca Loop from Laguna de los Peces, summiting the highest peak in Zamora and Galicia for panoramic views.
region_intro: Hiking around Galende is characterized by its glacial landscapes within the Sanabria Lake Natural Park, featuring the Iberian Peninsula's largest glacial lake. The region includes the Segundera and Cabrera mountains, with peaks exceeding two thousand meters, and is shaped by the Tera River and deep canyons. Oak forests, alongside other deciduous trees, and impressive waterfalls like Sotillo, define the natural environment. These features create a diverse terrain suitable for various hiking experiences. Best hiking trails around Galende The most popular hiking route is Lago de Sanabria via the Monjes path — circular through the Lago de Sanabria natural park and the Segundera and Porto mountain ranges, a 8.6 miles (13.9 km) trail that takes 4 hours 19 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of Lake Sanabria and winds through the Segundera and Porto mountain ranges. Another top favourite among local hikers is Trail of San Martín Cave and Tera Canyon, a difficult 9.2 miles (14.8 km) path. This trail traverses glacial landscapes and oak forests, gaining over 600 meters in elevation. Local hikers also love the Puebla de Sanabria from Puente — circular by the traditional path, a 7.2 miles (11.5 km) trail leading through traditional paths and scenic countryside, often completed in about 3 hours 3 minutes. Hiking around Galende is defined by glacial lakes, deep canyons, oak forests, and mountain ranges. The extensive network of trails offers options for various ability levels, from easy walks to challenging ascents. The routes in Galende are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 130 reviews. More than 1000 hikers have used komoot to explore Galende's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Galende?
A: Galende, situated within the Sanabria Lake Natural Park, offers a diverse network of over 50 hiking trails. These routes cater to various fitness levels, from easy strolls to challenging ascents through glacial landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes around Galende?
A: The terrain around Galende is characterized by its unique glacial origins. You'll encounter magnificent glacial lakes, deep valleys, moraines, and dramatic canyons like those of the Tera, Forcadura, Cárdena, and Segundera. The trails often wind through ancient oak forests, alongside rivers, and ascend into the Segundera and Cabrera mountains, offering varied and scenic landscapes.

Q: Are there any trails that feature waterfalls in Galende?
A: Yes, the region is home to impressive waterfalls. A notable example is the Sotillo Waterfall, which plunges approximately 20 meters and is particularly striking after rainfall. There are trails that lead to this and other cascades, often combined with visits to glacial lagoons.

Q: What is the best time of year for hiking in Galende?
A: The Sanabria Lake Natural Park is beautiful year-round. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and vibrant colors, with waterfalls at their most impressive after rainfall. Summer is ideal for combining hiking with water sports on Sanabria Lake, while winter can provide a unique, serene experience for those prepared for colder conditions.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Galende?
A: Yes, Galende offers several easy hiking trails suitable for families. These routes often feature gentler gradients and shorter distances, allowing everyone to enjoy the natural beauty of the Sanabria Lake Natural Park. For example, there are 6 easy routes available, perfect for a leisurely family outing.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Galende?
A: Many trails in the Sanabria Lake Natural Park are dog-friendly, allowing you to explore the beautiful landscapes with your canine companion. However, it's always recommended to keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or near livestock, and to check specific trail regulations before you go.

Q: Are there circular hiking routes available in Galende?
A: Yes, Galende features several excellent circular hiking routes. A popular choice is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1021981645/lago-de-sanabria-via-the-monjes-path-circular-through-the-lago-de-sanabria-natural-park-and-the-segundera-and-porto-mountain-ranges" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lago de Sanabria via the Monjes path&lt;/a&gt;, which offers stunning views of the lake and winds through the Segundera and Porto mountain ranges. Another option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1328190167/laguna-de-los-peces-y-laguna-de-yeguas-circular-por-la-senda-de-la-laguna-de-yeguas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna de los Peces y Laguna de Yeguas&lt;/a&gt;, a circular route exploring two beautiful glacial lakes.

Q: What are some interesting landmarks or attractions to see near the hiking trails?
A: Beyond the natural beauty, you can explore several historical and cultural landmarks. The Romanesque San Martín de Castañeda Monastery is an essential visit. Additionally, you might consider visiting the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/910086" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of the Counts of Benavente&lt;/a&gt; or the charming &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3834068" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Town of Puebla de Sanabria&lt;/a&gt;, both offering a glimpse into the region's rich history.

Q: Are there any mountain shelters or huts along the trails in Galende?
A: Yes, for those undertaking longer hikes or multi-day excursions, there are several mountain shelters and huts available. These include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3306935" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vega de Conde Shelter&lt;/a&gt;, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5423541" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cárdena Mountain Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5423588" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Pedro Refuge&lt;/a&gt;, providing essential rest stops in the wilderness.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about hiking in Galende?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 130 reviews. Reviewers often praise the stunning glacial landscapes, the tranquility of the oak forests, and the panoramic views of Sanabria Lake. The diversity of trails, from riverside walks to challenging mountain ascents, is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there any trails that lead through unique forests?
A: Yes, the region is home to the renowned &lt;strong&gt;Bosque del Tejedelo&lt;/strong&gt;, a unique forest known for its ancient yew trees and special atmosphere. There is a dedicated circular hiking route that allows visitors to explore this remarkable natural heritage near Requejo de Sanabria.

Q: Can I combine hiking with swimming or other water activities?
A: Absolutely! Sanabria Lake is the largest glacial lake on the Iberian Peninsula and offers various water sports. After a hike, you can enjoy bathing, canoeing, pedaloing, or catamaran navigation on the lake. Many trails offer direct access to the lake's shores, making it easy to cool off.
tips: Beautiful panoramic view of the Vega del Conde Reservoir from the GR-84 path in the Sanabria Lake and Segundera and Porto Natural Park
Located in a spectacular mountain setting, the Rio Pedro refuge is equipped with bunk beds, a fireplace, tables and chairs.

Located to the east of the province of Ourense, the Pena Trevinca mountains are a protected natural space that occupies almost 25,000 hectares. Its peaks, over 2,000 m high and snow-capped in winter, are crowned by glacial lakes such as O Celo or Lagoa da Serpe, with their purest, crystalline waters.

faqs.question: How long does the Peña Trevinca Loop from Laguna de los Peces typically take?
faqs.answer: This is a challenging hike. The full loop can take anywhere from 9 to 11 hours to complete, covering a distance of approximately 24 to 31 kilometers, depending on the exact variation you choose.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this hike?
faqs.answer: The trail conveniently starts near the picturesque Laguna de los Peces, which offers ample parking. This is a popular and accessible starting point for the route.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the Peña Trevinca Loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain varies significantly. You'll start on broad moorland with low vegetation like broom and heather. As you ascend, it transitions to a rugged, high-mountain environment with scarce vegetation and no forests. Expect glacial valleys, U-shaped formations, and the impressive Tera Valley. The final 2 kilometers to the summit are particularly steep and rugged, sometimes requiring the use of hands for stability.

faqs.question: What are the main viewpoints and landmarks along the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable points. You'll get views of the Vega del Conde Reservoir. The Río Pedro Refuge is also along the way. The ultimate highlight is the Peña Trevinca Summit, offering expansive panoramic views of the surrounding mountains and the entire Tera River valley, including peaks like Peña Negra and Peña Surbia.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to hike this route?
faqs.answer: The optimal time for this hike is typically in late spring or early summer. However, it is feasible year-round for well-prepared individuals with appropriate gear and experience, especially if you anticipate winter conditions like snow and ice.

faqs.question: Is the Peña Trevinca Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in the Parque Natural Lago de Sanabria y Sierras Segundera y de Porto, it's crucial to keep them on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure their safety on this challenging, high-mountain terrain. Be aware of potential water crossings and rugged sections that might be difficult for some dogs.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this hike?
faqs.answer: No specific hiking permits or entrance fees are generally required to access the trails within the Parque Natural Lago de Sanabria y Sierras Segundera y de Porto for day hikes. However, if you plan any specific activities like overnight camping, you should check with the park authorities for any regulations or permits needed.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations for hiking in the Parque Natural Lago de Sanabria y Sierras Segundera y de Porto?
faqs.answer: Yes, as this trail is within a natural park, it's important to follow park regulations. This typically includes staying on marked trails, not disturbing wildlife or plants, carrying out all your trash, and respecting any signage regarding protected areas. Always keep dogs on a leash.

faqs.question: Does this trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route incorporates sections of several other paths. You'll find yourself on parts of the "Ruta a Peña Trevinca (desde la Laguna de los Peces, Sanabria)", and significant portions of the "Sendero Laguna de los Peces - Peña Trevinca GR 84". It also briefly touches on "Montaña de Sanabria - Etapa 2" and "Montaña de Sanabria - Etapa 3".

faqs.question: What should I be aware of regarding water and weather conditions?
faqs.answer: The trail can be very wet, especially after rain or snowmelt, leading to waterlogged areas and challenging stream crossings. Always check the weather forecast before you go, as high-mountain conditions can change rapidly. Snow and ice can be present in certain seasons, requiring appropriate gear like crampons.

faqs.question: Are there any alternative or extended routes for more advanced hikers?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. While the direct loop is already challenging, adventurous hikers can extend the journey to incorporate additional peaks such as Peña Negra, Peña Surbia, and El Picón. These variations will significantly increase the length and difficulty, often involving exposed ridges with considerable drops.

waypoints.name: View of the Vega del Conde Reservoir
